title: Activism Burnout: When the Rage Turns Inward
dek: Activism burnout is a pervasive challenge faced by many passionate advocates who channel their anger toward social justice causes. When this intense rage turns inward, it can lead to emotional exhaustion, self-doubt, and a crisis of purpose. Understanding how to navigate these inner struggles is essential for sustaining long-term commitment and preserving mental well-being.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Body’s Ledger: When Rage Turns Inward


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:42.967Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

You know the feeling by now, that simmering coil beneath your ribs, the one that used to fuel your fire for justice but now feels like a slow, clenching vice inside your chest. It’s late, maybe the witching hour between two and four in the morning, when the world outside is quiet but your body is loud in its own language. Your diaphragm, that domed muscle just beneath your lungs, tightens as if it’s bracing for a blow. Each breath doesn’t expand your belly like it used to; instead, it feels shallow, trapped, like the air itself resists filling your lungs. This is the body’s ledger of activism burnout, and it’s written in muscle and fascia, not just in your mind.

Rage, when it’s turned outward, commands your body with a clarity that’s fierce. Your trapezius muscles - those sweeping, kite-shaped bands from the base of your skull over your shoulders - pull taut, readying you to confront the world. The levator scapulae, those thin muscles running alongside your neck, lift and tense, preparing your head for battle. But when rage turns inward, these muscles don’t release; they contract into a slow, grinding tension that becomes part of your somatic landscape, a landscape you carry into every moment, whether you want to or not.

## The Jaw Remembers

If you tilt your head slightly and place your fingers over your masseter muscles, you’ll find the hard, stubborn band of muscle that clenches when rage is swallowed. This muscle, responsible for chewing, becomes an unyielding fortress when fury is locked inside. You might not even realize you’re grinding your teeth until the morning light reveals the ache radiating from your jawline, a dull but persistent throb that no amount of painkillers can touch. The fascia around the masseter thickens, like a web hardened by years of unspoken words and swallowed shouts. Tension here is not pathology; it’s biology - it’s your rage’s footprint in flesh.

This clenched jaw is the body’s way of holding rage steady when it can’t find a voice outside. It’s what herbalist and somatic teacher Liz Koch calls “the jaw remembering.” You carry every unexpressed “enough” in that muscle, every moment you bit back your fury because the world told you it was ugly, unladylike, dangerous. Your body remembers the times you tried to scream but only tightened your throat and swallowed the sound. This muscle memory is a testament, a ledger entry of every fight you internalized.

## The Psoas: Your Core of Containment

Deeper inside, beneath the abdominal wall, lies the psoas muscle - a thick, powerful muscle running alongside your lumbar spine and attaching to your femur. It’s the body’s primal core, the place where your fight-or-flight response first manifests. When your rage turns inward, the psoas contracts and holds fast, like a clenched fist in your belly. This muscle doesn’t just tighten; it becomes a fortress of containment, protecting you from the emotional onslaught but also trapping you in a state of chronic tension.

You might notice this as a persistent ache or a dull heaviness in your lower back or hips, a sensation that no amount of stretching or yoga can fully dissolve. The fascia surrounding the psoas thickens with this chronic contraction, creating a tight, resistant sheath that limits your capacity to move freely, to breathe fully, to feel safe in your body. The psoas remembers every moment you absorbed the world’s injustices without outlet, every time you bent beneath the weight of systemic rage and internalized it as personal failure or exhaustion.

## The Fatigue of the Fascia

Fascia, that intricate web of connective tissue encasing every muscle, nerve, and organ, is often overlooked in conversations about burnout. Yet it is precisely this tissue that records the body’s emotional history. Unlike muscles, fascia doesn’t contract and relax in the same way. It thickens, hardens, and loses elasticity in response to chronic tension and stress. When activism burnout sets in, the fascia around your trapezius, levator scapulae, and psoas becomes a dense, resistant sheath. You might feel it as a persistent tightness or a dull, aching rigidity beneath your skin.

This fascial fatigue is not just physical; it’s an energetic bottleneck. The body’s fluid communication slows, and you feel trapped inside your own skin, as if your very tissues conspire to hold you hostage in a constant state of alert. This is not weakness or failure. It is the body’s aching, unrelenting record of sustained fury, a testament to your endurance and your pain.

## The Smell of Midnight Sweat and Herbal Resolve

In the thick of burnout, your body smells different, too. Maybe it’s the metallic tang of adrenal exhaustion, the faint bitterness of cortisol hanging in the air around your skin. Maybe it’s the heavy, musky scent of sweat pooling in the small of your back or beneath your arms during a restless night. This smell is not shame; it is evidence of your body’s relentless labor, the somatic toll of carrying invisible burdens.

You might reach for herbs - perhaps a tincture of skullcap or a decoction of rehmannia - but these are not magic escapes. They are serious medicines, allies in the slow work of unwinding muscle tension, softening fascia, and retraining the nervous system to breathe fully again. As herbalist Matthew Wood often reminds his students, the body knows what it needs; your role is to listen deeply to the signals encoded in muscle tightness and tissue fatigue.

## When Rage Finds No Outlet

Activism burnout isn’t just mental exhaustion or emotional depletion. It is a somatic crisis, a biological state where the body’s natural defenses against stress become pathways of chronic pain and tension. When rage turns inward, it doesn’t disappear; it embeds itself in the masseter’s grinding, the psoas’s iron grip, the trapezius’s constant shrug of readiness. It thickens your fascia, contracts your diaphragm, and steals your breath in the middle of the night.

This is not a failure of spirit or will. This is biology. And as biology, it insists on being acknowledged, understood, and treated with the seriousness of a healing art that honors the body’s wisdom. You are not alone in this. The body remembers, and so do you.

## The Body Holds the Score: Resmaa Menakem’s Somatic Wisdom

You know the weight that settles in your chest, the tightness crawling up your neck, the way your trapezius and levator scapulae lock into a silent siege at 2 a.m., when the world has quieted down but your mind hasn’t. That’s not just stress; that’s the body’s archive of rage and trauma, folding inward when you can’t express it outwardly. Resmaa Menakem, a somatic therapist and author of *My Grandmother’s Hands*, teaches us that trauma lives not just in the mind but in the body’s tissues - muscles, fascia, even the bones themselves. The jaw remembers the clenches, the diaphragm remembers the shallow breaths, the psoas remembers the fight-or-flight readiness. This is not pathology, this is biology.

Menakem’s work is revolutionary because it refuses to separate the political from the physical. When your fury at injustice turns inward, it’s not just emotional exhaustion; it’s your nervous system caught in a feedback loop of hypervigilance and shutdown. He insists we must engage the body directly - through movement, breath, touch - not just talk about feelings. The body knows. Your masseter muscle may twitch under the weight of unspoken words; your diaphragm may hold a tight knot that blocks full inhale and exhale, leaving you gasping in your sleep or waking with a dry, metallic taste on your tongue. This is the language your body uses to tell you: you are holding too much.

Menakem’s somatic approach invites you to practice what he calls “grounding” in the flesh of your own experience. It’s not spiritual fluff but a serious practice of noticing how your bones and muscles carry the history of your rage and your resilience. When you slow down to feel the tension in your neck, the way your trapezius curls protectively around your shoulders, or the quiver that runs along the fascia of your forearms, you reclaim power from the burnout. You learn to discharge the rage safely, rather than letting it erode you from the inside out.

## Pema Chödrön: Befriending the Edge of Your Rage

If Menakem invites you into the body, Pema Chödrön offers a way to befriend the raging river running beneath your skin. The American Buddhist nun and author of *When Things Fall Apart* teaches that fury, even the fury that burns you, is not your enemy. It is a raw, honest signal that something needs attention. In Pema’s view, the impulse to turn your rage inward - to hit yourself with blame, shame, or exhaustion - is an old habit that asks for mindfulness and compassion, not judgment.

She writes about the importance of “leaning into discomfort,” which feels like trying to hold a live coal in your hands without burning yourself. This is where the body’s wisdom and Pema’s teaching meet. When your masseter tightens and your breath shortens, instead of resisting or numbing the sensation, you learn to observe it with a tender curiosity. What does this tightness want to tell you? What is the story held in your diaphragm’s shallow rise and fall? This practice is not about forcing calm but about creating a safe container for the chaos.

Pema’s teaching reminds you that rage is not a sign of failure; it is a form of vitality. The exhaustion that follows activism burnout is often the body’s way of saying, “I have carried this fire too long without rest.” Instead of pushing through with caffeine or willpower, she encourages a radical acceptance of your limits. This is not surrender; it is a strategic regrouping. When you accept the edge of your rage, you open a space where healing can begin - not through denial or repression but through intimate acquaintance with your own aliveness.

## Herbal Allies: The Serious Medicine of Plants

In the late hours when your diaphragm feels like a tight band wrapped around your ribs and your masseter muscle clenches so hard it sends sparks to your temple, herbs can be potent allies. This is not about tea-time relaxation or trendy tinctures; this is about serious medicine that honors the complexity of your body’s signals.

Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) recognizes the liver as the organ tied most closely to the free flow of qi - or life energy - and, by extension, to the management of rage and frustration. When the liver qi stagnates, you feel irritable, tense, and prone to headaches or digestive upset. This stagnation often shows in the physical tension of your neck and shoulders, the tightness in your jaw, the restless tossing in bed at 3 a.m. TCM approaches this with herbs that move the liver qi, calm the spirit, and nourish the blood.

One such formula is Xiao Yao San, sometimes called Free and Easy Wanderer, which gently soothes liver qi stagnation and supports emotional balance. The combination of bupleurum root, peony, and angelica in this formula works to loosen the fascia and muscle restrictions that hold your rage in place. It’s not a sedative; it’s a mover of stuckness. You might find this formula in tincture or capsule form at an herbalist’s dispensary or in carefully sourced products like [Solaray Total Calm Advanced](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000Z1I5EC?tag=spankyspinola-20), trusted for its purity and potency.

Western herbalism offers its own allies. Skullcap and passionflower, for example, work on the nervous system to ease the overactive fight-or-flight response. They soothe the diaphragm and the vagus nerve, inviting a deeper, more restorative breath. Chamomile and lavender, while more familiar, play a role in quieting the mind and softening the physical tension of chronic rage held in the masseter and trapezius. But these herbs are not magic fixes; they require the same respect and attention you give your body’s other needs - rest, movement, and honest reflection.

## The Slow Work of Reclaiming Your Body

Activism burnout is a slow unraveling of the body’s defenses. It’s the psoas muscle holding tension from the primal urge to fight, the levator scapulae bracing to protect a vulnerable neck, the diaphragm caught in a cage of fear and frustration. You don’t fix this with a pep talk or a quick vacation; you fix it with the slow, steady work of listening.

You might start by noticing the subtle twitching in your masseter as you reread that email that set your teeth on edge. You might lie down at dawn, feeling the weight of your ribs rising and falling, smelling the cool morning air from an open window. You might take a carefully brewed tincture, feeling the earthy bitterness slide down your throat, grounding you in the moment.

The teachers and the herbs remind you: this is not pathology, this is biology. Your body is remembering what your mind has tried to forget. Your rage is not ugly; it is a vital force that demands movement. Your exhaustion is not weakness; it is a signal to pause and tend to the muscles and fascia that carry your story.

When you tend to these muscles, when you honor the wisdom in your diaphragm and masseter, you begin to reclaim your power - not by pushing harder but by listening deeper. That is the real work of healing activism burnout.

## When the Rage Turns Inward: The Body’s Silent Rebellion

You know the feeling well: the fire that once roared through your chest, fueling your activism, begins to smolder into a slow, corrosive burn. It’s not just exhaustion; it’s a betrayal felt deep in the tissues. The trapezius muscles, those heavy bands running from your neck to your shoulders, knot into tight little fists. Your diaphragm, normally a fluid and responsive muscle beneath your ribs, clamps down in a shallow, stilted breath pattern. This is not pathology, this is biology. The body knows the cost of carrying rage that turns inward.

When your fury is denied expression, it retreats into the dark corners of your jaw - the masseter muscle clenched so tightly it feels like stone grinding on stone. You wake at 3 a.m., the levator scapulae muscles along the sides of your neck screaming with tension, your breath trapped beneath a locked chest. Your skin smells faintly of sweat and dried salt, the remnants of tears you didn’t let fall. This is the body's silent rebellion against the betrayal of your own emotional landscape. It is the body’s way of saying: “I am still here, still aching, still resisting.”

This inward turn of rage is a quiet killer of hope and vitality. It is what exhausts your spirit when every external fight seems endless, when every victory is shadowed by the next looming defeat. You were taught that fury was ugly, that your rage made you less, and so you swallowed it down, tightening your psoas muscles, the deep core stabilizers that hold your spine and pelvis together. Like a tightly wound spring, your core holds the weight of suppressed fury, ready to snap at the slightest provocation.

## Releasing the Rage in the Body: An Embodied Practice

There is a way back through this, through the body’s own wisdom. It begins with a practice of releasing the rage held in your tissues, a slow and deliberate unwinding of the tension that has become your second skin. Find a quiet space where you won’t be disturbed. Lie on your back on a firm surface, your knees bent and your feet flat on the floor. Place one hand on your belly, just below your navel, and the other on your chest, over your heart.

Close your eyes and bring your attention to your breath. Notice how shallow it feels, how the diaphragm resists expanding. Do not force it. Simply observe the tightness, the way your ribs feel locked in place. Now, bring your awareness to your jaw. Feel the masseter muscle, the hard knot of tension there. Gently part your lips, allowing your jaw to drop slightly. Feel the space between your teeth widen.

As you breathe in, imagine the breath traveling down into your belly, softening the psoas muscle. As you exhale, let out a slow, audible sigh. This is not a polite sigh; let it be a sound of release, a vocalization of the tension you are holding. Repeat this for several minutes, allowing the sound to grow louder if it needs to. You are not trying to fix anything; you are simply giving your body permission to express what it has been holding. This is the beginning of reclaiming your somatic landscape.

## FAQ: Navigating Activism Burnout

**Q: How do I know if my activism burnout is physical or just emotional?**

**A:** The body knows before the mind does. If you are experiencing chronic tension in your jaw, neck, or shoulders, if your breath is consistently shallow, or if you wake up feeling exhausted despite a full night’s sleep, your burnout has become physical. Emotional exhaustion often manifests as apathy or irritability, but somatic burnout is felt in the tissues - the masseter, the trapezius, the psoas.

**Q: Can herbs really help with the physical symptoms of burnout?**

**A:** Yes, but they are not quick fixes. Herbs like skullcap, passionflower, and formulas like Xiao Yao San work to support the nervous system and move stagnant energy. They are serious medicines that require consistent use and a willingness to listen to your body’s signals. They work best when combined with somatic practices and adequate rest.

**Q: I feel guilty taking time away from my activism. How do I reconcile this?**

**A:** This is a common struggle, but remember that your body is the instrument of your activism. If the instrument is broken, the work cannot continue. Taking time to heal is not a betrayal of your cause; it is a necessary act of self-preservation. As Pema Chödrön teaches, leaning into the discomfort of rest is a radical act of compassion for yourself and, ultimately, for the world you are trying to change.

**Q: What if I can’t find a quiet space for the embodied practice?**

**A:** You don’t need a perfect environment to begin. Even a few minutes in your car before walking into work, or sitting on the edge of your bed before sleep, can be enough. The key is consistency and intention. Focus on softening your jaw and deepening your breath, even if it’s just for three conscious inhales and exhales. Every small act of somatic awareness is a step toward healing.

---
